scenes.title: How Python's GIL Works
scenes.subtitle: Why Python threads don't always run in parallel.
scenes.label: Scene 1 / 10
stepSubtitles: Python Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) Guide.
Understanding thread serialization and execution constraints.
Press play or next to start.

scenes.title: The Intuition
scenes.subtitle: Multiple threads compete for a single lock before executing Python code.
scenes.label: Scene 2 / 10
stepSubtitles: Defines a CPU-bound compute function task.
Thread A is instantiated to run the computation task.
Thread B is instantiated to run the computation task in parallel.
Both threads must compile and execute bytecode via the CPython interpreter.
However, the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) acts as a single gateway.

scenes.title: What Is the GIL?
scenes.subtitle: A mutex that prevents multiple threads from executing Python bytecode at once.
scenes.label: Scene 3 / 10
stepSubtitles: Thread A attempts to acquire the GIL to execute bytecode.
Thread B is forced to wait as the GIL is already held by Thread A.
Result: Only one thread executes Python bytecode at any given moment.

scenes.title: Thread Switching
scenes.subtitle: The GIL is periodically released and re-acquired, allowing thread interleaving.
scenes.label: Scene 4 / 10
stepSubtitles: Thread 1 acquires the GIL and executes instruction: 'x += 1'.
Thread 1 finishes executing the first instruction.
Thread 1 continues execution with GIL: 'y += x'.
Thread 1 finishes executing the second instruction.
Thread 1 continues execution with GIL: 'z += y'.
Thread 1 finishes executing the third instruction.
CPython forces context switch: Thread 1 releases the GIL, Thread 2 acquires the lock.
Thread 2 begins bytecode execution under GIL: 'a += 1'.
Thread 2 finishes executing the first instruction.
Thread 2 continues execution with GIL: 'b += a'.
Thread 2 finishes executing the second instruction.
Execution cycles show that concurrency is achieved via time-slicing, not true parallelism.

scenes.title: CPU-Bound Work
scenes.subtitle: Compute-heavy threads waste time waiting for the GIL instead of running in parallel.
scenes.label: Scene 5 / 10
stepSubtitles: A CPU-heavy loop runs, keeping the CPU cores saturated.
Threads repeatedly preempt each other every 5ms (or 100 ticks), context-switching the GIL between cores.
Thread contention on the GIL leads to high CPU usage with poor performance gains.

scenes.title: Why Does the GIL Exist?
scenes.subtitle: It protects shared interpreter state and simplifies reference counting.
scenes.label: Scene 6 / 10
stepSubtitles: Thread 1 tries to access interpreter reference count tables.
Thread 2 concurrently tries to modify reference counts for garbage collection.
Thread 3 concurrently tries to access shared objects.
To prevent race conditions, the GIL is locked, serializing access to CPython internals.
This guarantees safe object reference counting and memory stability.
It also makes integrating C-extensions extremely straightforward and stable.

scenes.title: I/O Changes Everything
scenes.subtitle: The GIL is released during blocking I/O, allowing other threads to run.
scenes.label: Scene 7 / 10
stepSubtitles: A thread initiates a network request using the popular requests library.
While running CPU instructions, the thread holds the GIL.
When the network call blocks (waiting for response), the GIL is voluntarily released.
With the GIL released, Thread B immediately acquires the lock and starts running bytecode.
Thread B executes its computation until Thread A receives the network data response.
This cooperative yielding enables effective concurrency during I/O block periods.

scenes.title: Why Threads Still Help
scenes.subtitle: Threads improve throughput for I/O-bound workloads despite the GIL.
scenes.label: Scene 8 / 10
stepSubtitles: Multiple threads initiate downloading resources concurrently.
Threads release the GIL during network wait, utilizing idle time efficiently.
Execution segments show threads executing code while others wait for I/O packets.
Thus, threads significantly improve execution throughput for I/O-bound tasks.

scenes.title: Multiprocessing
scenes.subtitle: Each process gets its own interpreter and GIL, enabling true parallelism.
scenes.label: Scene 9 / 10
stepSubtitles: Before: A single OS process runs one CPython interpreter with one GIL.
Transitioning to a multi-process architecture.
After: Spawning multiple processes, each running its own CPython interpreter.
Process A executes bytecode on CPU Core 1 with its own local GIL.
Process B executes bytecode on CPU Core 2 with its own local GIL.
Process C executes bytecode on CPU Core 3 with its own local GIL.
This bypasses the single GIL limit, fully utilizing multiple CPU cores in parallel.

scenes.title: Final Summary
scenes.subtitle: The GIL is a design trade-off between simplicity and parallelism.
scenes.label: Scene 10 / 10
stepSubtitles: CPU-Bound tasks: Serialized execution due to a single GIL results in no speedup.
I/O-Bound tasks: GIL is released during wait times, enabling concurrency.
Multiprocessing: Spawns independent interpreters, enabling true hardware parallelism.
The CPython concurrency design matrix summary.
The GIL remains a fundamental pillar of CPython reference-counting internals.
